name: Accessibility & Inclusivity Standards
purpose: Ensures the cafe is welcoming and accessible to all customers regardless of physical ability, sensory needs, dietary requirements, neurodivergence, or other characteristics. This block covers physical access, communication, menu inclusivity, and the cultural mindset required to make every customer feel genuinely welcome.

detailed_objectives: Audit the venue for physical accessibility barriers and create an improvement plan
Establish clear communication standards for customers with hearing, visual, or cognitive needs
Ensure menu information is available in accessible formats
Train all staff on inclusive service practices and disability awareness
Create a welcoming environment for neurodivergent customers and families with additional needs
secondary_objectives: Comply with the Equality Act 2010 duty to make reasonable adjustments
Expand the customer base by removing barriers that exclude potential visitors
Build a reputation as a genuinely inclusive venue that attracts loyal advocacy
Ensure digital presence (website, social media) meets basic accessibility standards

why_this_matters: The Equality Act 2010 places a legal duty on all UK service providers to make reasonable adjustments for disabled people. This is not optional — it is the law. But beyond legal compliance, accessibility and inclusivity represent a significant commercial opportunity that most cafes overlook entirely. Approximately 14.1 million people in the UK are disabled, representing over 20% of the population and commanding a combined spending power known as the Purple Pound, estimated at over £274 billion annually. Research by the charity Scope found that 75% of disabled people and their families have walked away from a business because of poor accessibility or attitudes. Each time a wheelchair user cannot enter your cafe, a deaf customer cannot communicate their order, or an autistic visitor is overwhelmed by an unpredictable environment, you lose not just that customer but their entire social group. Inclusivity also extends beyond disability: parents with pushchairs, elderly customers with mobility aids, people with temporary injuries, non-English speakers, and customers with dietary needs driven by faith or medical conditions. A cafe that is thoughtfully inclusive attracts a wider customer base, generates stronger loyalty, and earns genuine word-of-mouth advocacy from communities that are deeply underserved by the hospitality industry. The reputational benefit of being known as the cafe that truly welcomes everyone is immeasurable. Getting this wrong, conversely, risks public criticism, legal complaints, and lasting damage to your brand.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Conduct a basic accessibility walk-through of the cafe. Approach the venue as someone using a wheelchair: Can you get through the door? Is there a step? Can you reach the counter? Can you access a table? Can you reach the toilet? Note every barrier. Repeat the exercise considering a visually impaired customer, a deaf customer, and a parent with a double pushchair. Check the basics: Is the entrance at least 800mm wide? Is there a clear path to the counter without furniture obstacles? Are tables at a height accessible to wheelchair users (at least one table should be)? Is the toilet accessible or is there a reasonable alternative nearby? Create a prioritised list of barriers, separating them into 'fix this week' (moving furniture, adding signage), 'fix this month' (portable ramp, menu boards), and 'longer-term investment' (structural changes, accessible toilet). Week 2: Brief the team on inclusive service basics. Cover: how to offer assistance without being patronising ('Would you like any help?' rather than grabbing someone's wheelchair), how to communicate with customers who are deaf or hard of hearing (face them, speak clearly, offer to write things down), how to be patient with customers who need more time to order. Ensure the menu is available in a large-print format (minimum 14pt font) and that staff can describe menu items clearly for customers who cannot read the board. Add a note to your Google Business listing confirming the cafe's accessibility features — or honestly stating current limitations.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a formal Accessibility Policy document that outlines the cafe's commitment, current provisions, and improvement plan. This document demonstrates compliance with the Equality Act 2010 duty to make reasonable adjustments. Install any quick-win physical improvements: a portable ramp if there is a step, a hearing loop sticker if applicable, tactile strips on step edges, contrasting colours on glass doors. Create an accessible menu — large print, clear font (sans-serif, minimum 14pt), high contrast, available in a format customers can hold rather than only displayed on a wall board. Consider producing a picture menu for customers with learning disabilities or limited English. Train all staff through a formal disability awareness session covering the social model of disability, the Equality Act duty, communication techniques, and specific scenarios they may encounter. Establish a sensory-friendly protocol: during quieter periods, offer a lower-stimulation environment (reduced music volume, dimmed lighting where possible) for neurodivergent customers or those with sensory processing needs. Review your digital presence: does your website meet basic WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards? Can someone using a screen reader navigate your online menu? Add alt text to images on social media. Create a feedback mechanism specifically for accessibility — a way for customers to tell you about barriers they have encountered without feeling awkward.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Inclusivity should now be woven into every decision the cafe makes. When choosing new furniture, ask 'Is this accessible?' When designing a new menu board, ask 'Can everyone read this?' When planning an event, ask 'Who might be excluded?' Conduct a formal annual accessibility audit using a structured checklist, ideally with input from disabled customers or local disability organisations. Pursue recognition such as the Disability Confident employer scheme or local accessibility awards. Introduce a 'hidden disabilities' sunflower lanyard awareness programme so staff recognise when a customer may need additional patience or assistance. Establish partnerships with local disability groups, autism support networks, or care homes to actively invite underserved communities. Monitor and respond to accessibility-related feedback on review platforms. Share your accessibility journey transparently on social media — customers respect honesty about limitations combined with genuine effort to improve. Review and refresh staff training annually, incorporating lessons from real interactions and feedback.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Conduct an accessibility walk-through considering wheelchair, visual, hearing, and pushchair access
Create a prioritised barrier list categorised by fix timeframe
Implement immediate zero-cost fixes (furniture rearrangement, clear pathways)
Brief the team on inclusive service basics and appropriate language
Produce a large-print menu (minimum 14pt, high contrast, sans-serif font)
Update Google Business listing with accurate accessibility information
Ensure at least one table is wheelchair-accessible without requiring furniture to be moved

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a formal Accessibility Policy document demonstrating Equality Act compliance
Install physical accessibility improvements (portable ramp, tactile strips, contrast markings)
Create accessible menu formats including large print and picture menu
Deliver formal disability awareness training to all staff
Establish a sensory-friendly protocol for quieter periods
Audit digital presence for basic WCAG 2.1 compliance
Create a dedicated accessibility feedback mechanism

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct annual formal accessibility audit with external input
Pursue Disability Confident or similar accreditation
Introduce hidden disabilities sunflower lanyard awareness programme
Establish partnerships with local disability and community organisations
Integrate accessibility considerations into all new purchases and design decisions
Share accessibility journey transparently on social media and marketing

deliverables: Accessible Menu (large print and picture formats)

tools.description: Structured checklist covering physical access (entrance, pathways, counter, seating, toilets), communication (menu formats, staff interaction), sensory environment, and digital presence. Used for initial audit and annual reviews.
Prioritised list of accessibility barriers identified during the audit, categorised by fix timeframe (immediate, this month, longer-term) with assigned responsibility and completion status.
Training materials covering the social model of disability, Equality Act 2010 duties, communication techniques for different needs, scenario-based role-plays, and appropriate language guidance.
Simple feedback form (physical and digital) specifically for customers to report accessibility barriers or suggest improvements, designed to be low-pressure and available in accessible formats.
Comprehensive annual report documenting current accessibility provisions, improvements made, remaining barriers, customer feedback, staff training completion, and next year's improvement plan.

failure_modes: The accessibility audit is conducted but no improvements are actually made, creating a paper exercise with no real impact
Staff training focuses on rules rather than empathy, resulting in technically correct but emotionally cold interactions with disabled customers
Physical improvements are made but communication barriers remain — a ramp is installed but staff still do not know how to interact with a deaf customer
Accessibility is treated as a one-off project rather than an ongoing commitment, and standards slip over time as furniture is rearranged or new staff join untrained
kpis: 100% of staff complete disability awareness training within first month of employment
All identified 'fix this week' barriers resolved within 7 days of audit
Accessible menu formats available and up to date at all times
constraints: Some physical accessibility improvements require landlord permission in leased premises
Structural changes may require planning permission and significant capital investment
Staff training must be refreshed annually and included in onboarding for new starters

time_load.L1: 4 hours (walk-through audit, team briefing, immediate fixes, menu production)
time_load.L2: 12 hours (policy drafting, physical improvements, staff training, digital audit)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (annual audit, training refresh, partnership development)

review_protocol: Monthly check of barrier improvement progress. Quarterly review of accessibility feedback. Annual formal accessibility audit with external input. Staff training refreshed annually. Accessibility Policy reviewed and updated annually.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures the cafe is genuinely welcoming and accessible to all customers, covering physical access, communication, menu formats, sensory needs, and staff training. It addresses both legal compliance with the Equality Act 2010 and the commercial opportunity of serving the underserved.
implementation_risks: Physical limitations of older premises may make some improvements impossible without major investment
Staff may feel uncomfortable discussing disability if training is not handled sensitively
Overpromising accessibility on the Google listing when barriers still exist can create worse experiences than honest communication
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate accessibility complaints, low diversity in customer base, Equality Act compliance concerns, or when the venue is undergoing refurbishment (opportunity to improve access). Links closely to B036 journey mapping.
